name: Taniwha
description: Taniwha are powerful beings in Māori mythology, often described as monstrous creatures dwelling in deep pools, rivers, caves, or the sea. They can be benevolent guardians of people and places, or dangerous predators, depending on the context and the specific Taniwha. Their roles are multifaceted, encompassing protectors of tribal boundaries, enforcers of tapu (sacred restrictions), and even ancestral figures. Taniwha are deeply intertwined with the landscape and are considered a vital part of the natural world. Respecting and appeasing Taniwha is crucial for maintaining balance and harmony. Some Taniwha are shapeshifters, capable of taking on different forms, including those of animals or even humans. Their power (mana) is significant, and encounters with them require caution and reverence. They are often associated with specific locations, their presence shaping the cultural and spiritual significance of those places. Stories of Taniwha serve as important lessons about respecting the environment and adhering to cultural protocols.
subtitle: Powerful guardian or dangerous predator in Māori mythology.
appearance: Taniwha's appearance varies greatly depending on the specific story and region. They are often described as resembling large reptiles, such as lizards or crocodiles, or monstrous fish with sharp teeth and scales. Some are depicted with features of other creatures, including birds or even human-like faces. Their size can range from several meters long to enormous, mountain-sized beings. Coloration is also variable, with descriptions including green, brown, black, and even iridescent scales. Some accounts portray them as having glowing eyes and emitting a powerful aura.
habitat: Taniwha inhabit various aquatic environments, including deep pools in rivers, lakes, the open sea, caves near water sources, and sometimes even in dangerous rapids or waterfalls. They are strongly connected to specific locations, which are often considered sacred or tapu.
behavior: Taniwha can exhibit a range of behaviors. Some act as benevolent guardians, protecting local tribes and ensuring the safety of waterways. Others are malevolent, causing floods, capsizing canoes, and attacking people. Their behavior is often influenced by how they are treated. Respectful offerings and adherence to tapu can appease them, while disrespect or violation of sacred sites can provoke their wrath. Some Taniwha are considered to be ancestral figures, offering guidance and protection to their descendants.

keyMyths.title: Ngake and Whataitai
keyMyths.description: This story tells of two Taniwha, Ngake and Whataitai, who lived in the Wellington Harbour. Ngake, using his intelligence and strength, created the harbour's entrance, while Whataitai became stranded and transformed into a sandbar. This tale explains the geography of the harbour and highlights the power and agency of Taniwha.
keyMyths.source: Grey, George. *Polynesian Mythology and Ancient Traditional History of the New Zealand Race.*

keyMyths.title: Matau and the Formation of the Clutha River
keyMyths.description: The Taniwha Matau blocked the Clutha River (Mata-au), causing a great flood. A brave man, using fire, tricked and killed Matau, allowing the river to flow freely. The shape of the river is said to reflect the contortions of the dying Taniwha. This story illustrates the conflict between humans and Taniwha and the importance of resourcefulness.
keyMyths.source: Reed, A. W. *Reed Book of Māori Mythology.*

keyMyths.title: Kaiwhare as Guardian
keyMyths.description: The Taniwha Kaiwhare was a guardian of the Tainui waka (canoe) during its voyage to New Zealand. He protected the waka from storms and dangers at sea, demonstrating the benevolent role that Taniwha could play in protecting people and ensuring their safe passage. He is still revered as a protector by descendants of those who travelled on the Tainui waka.
keyMyths.source: Te Hurinui, Pei. *King Potatau: An Account of the Life of Potatau Te Wherowhero, the First Maori King.*
